coredata.dc:title: Multi-stimuli responsive copper nanoclusters with bright red luminescence for quantifying acid phosphatase activity via redox-controlled luminescence switch
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Meizhi
Feng, Hui
Han, Jiaonan
Ao, Hang
Qian, Zhaosheng
coredata.dc:description: Thiolate-protected copper nanoclusers (CuNCs) are emerging as a promising class of luminescent materials since its unique optical properties such as aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and intriguing molecular-like behavior have been explored for sensing application. In this work, multi-stimuli responsive property of CuNCs was first investigated in depth and further adopted to develop a reliable and sensitive ACP assay. Penicilamine-capped CuNCs from a facile one-pot synthesis possess bright red luminescence and distinctive multi-stimuli responsive behaviors. Its sensitive and reversible response in luminescence to pH and temperature is originated from its inherent AIE property, and can be constructed as luminescent nanoswitches controlled by these external stimuli for precisely monitoring the change of environmental pH or temperature. The specific redox-responsive behavior of CuNC aggregates is found from severe luminescence quenching in the presence of a small amount of ferric or silver ions, and this sensitive response in luminescence to the preceding species is proved to be due to the conversion of Cu(II) from copper atoms with lower valence inside CuNCs. The luminescence switch of CuNC aggregates controlled by specific external potentials is further utilized to design a novel detection strategy for ACP activity. The great difference in luminescence quenching of CuNCs induced by iron(III) pyrophosphate (FePPi2) complex and free ferric ions enables us to quantitatively monitor ACP level by the luminescence change as variation of ACP activity in the assay solution. This assay is able to detect ACP level as lower as 0.8 U/L, and covers a broad linear scope of 100.0 U/L. This work reports redox-responsive property of CuNCs and its underlying nature due to the oxidation of its interior copper atoms, and provides a sensitive assay method for ACP activity which is sufficiently sensitive for practical measurement in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Acid phosphatase assay
Multi-stimuli responsive nanoswitches
Redox-controlled switch
Temperature and pH sensors

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CuNCs-based luminescence switches controlled by pH and temperature
Luminescence quenching of CuNCs by ferric ions
Principle of ACP level detection based on redox-responsive CuNC aggregates
AIE-based luminescent nanoswitches of CuNCs controlled by pH and temperature
Real-time luminescent assay for acid phosphatase based on redox-responsive CuNC aggregates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Luminescence excitation and emission spectra of CuNCs dispersion in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 5.0) and CuNCs powder. Inset: images of CuNCs dispersion (a) and CuNCs powder (b) under UV lamp. (B) Time-resolved luminescence decay curves of CuNCs dispersion and CuNCs powder, and their corresponding fitting curves and equations. (C) TEM image and high-resolution TEM image of CuNCs dispersion in water. (D) XPS wide survey spectrum of CuNCs.

(A) Luminescence spectra of CuNCs at variable pHs from 4.0 to 8.0. (B) Reversibility test of CuNCs controlled by pHs between 4.0 and 6.8. (C) Luminescence spectra of CuNCs at different temperatures in the range of 0–40 °C. (D) Reversibility test of CuNCs controlled by temperature between 0 °C and 40 °C.

(A) Change of luminescence emission spectra of CuNCs aggregate in buffer solution (HAc-NaAc, pH 5.0) in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ from 0.0 to 93.3 μM. (B) Value (I0/I) and (τ0/τ) vs the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0.0–25.0 μM. (C) Change of luminescence emission spectra of CuNCs aggregate (pH 5.0) upon addition of different components: (a) CuNCs aggregate (0.4 mg/mL); (b) CuNCs aggregate, and Fe3+ (93.3 μM); (c) CuNCs aggregate, Fe3+ (93.3 μM) and PPi (186.6 μM); (d) CuNCs aggregate, and Fe(PPi)2 – (93.3 μM). (D) The cyclic voltammetry curve of CuNCs aggregate in buffer solution (HAc-NaAc, pH 5.0), where the potential range is set from −0.4 V (vs SCE) to 0.6 V (vs SCE) using typical three-electrode system containing a glassy carbon electrode, a Pt wire electrode and a calomel electrode.
The change of luminescence spectra of CuNCs in solution (pH 5.0) containing 1.0 M NaCl when different oxidation potentials are applied: (A) 0.8 V vs SCE; (B) 0.4 V vs SCE; (C) 0.0 V vs SCE.

(A) Change of luminescence spectra of CuNCs (pH 5.0) in the presence of various components: (a) CuNCs (0.4 mg/mL); (b) CuNCs, and Fe3+ (93.3 μM); (c) CuNCs, and Fe(PPi)2 – (93.3 μM); (d) CuNCs, and phosphate ion (373.2 μM) and Fe3+ (93.3 μM); (e) CuNCs, Fe(PPi)2 – (93.3 μM) and ACP (380.0 U/L). (B) Luminescence intensity vs incubation time of the mixture containing CuNCs (0.4 mg/mL), Fe(PPi)2 – (93.3 μM), and ACP at different levels (180.0, and 380.0 U/L). (C) Luminescence spectra of the mixture containing CuNCs (0.4 mg/mL) and Fe(PPi)2 – (93.3 μM) at varied ACP levels from 0.0 to 380.0 U/L. (D) The luminescence intensity as the function of ACP activity. Inset: Fitting curve between quenching efficiency [(I0-I)/I0] and ACP activity in the range from 2.2 to 45.5 U/L. All the data have been repeated three times.
Schematic Illustration of Multi-stimuli Responsive CuNCs Nanoswitches and Detection Strategy for ACP Activity Based on Redox-Controlled Luminescence.

head.ce:title: Multi-stimuli responsive copper nanoclusters with bright red luminescence for quantifying acid phosphatase activity via redox-controlled luminescence switch

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiolate-protected copper nanoclusers (CuNCs) are emerging as a promising class of luminescent materials since its unique optical properties such as aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and intriguing molecular-like behavior have been explored for sensing application. In this work, multi-stimuli responsive property of CuNCs was first investigated in depth and further adopted to develop a reliable and sensitive ACP assay. Penicilamine-capped CuNCs from a facile one-pot synthesis possess bright red luminescence and distinctive multi-stimuli responsive behaviors. Its sensitive and reversible response in luminescence to pH and temperature is originated from its inherent AIE property, and can be constructed as luminescent nanoswitches controlled by these external stimuli for precisely monitoring the change of environmental pH or temperature. The specific redox-responsive behavior of CuNC aggregates is found from severe luminescence quenching in the presence of a small amount of ferric or silver ions, and this sensitive response in luminescence to the preceding species is proved to be due to the conversion of Cu(II) from copper atoms with lower valence inside CuNCs. The luminescence switch of CuNC aggregates controlled by specific external potentials is further utilized to design a novel detection strategy for ACP activity. The great difference in luminescence quenching of CuNCs induced by iron(III) pyrophosphate (FePPi2) complex and free ferric ions enables us to quantitatively monitor ACP level by the luminescence change as variation of ACP activity in the assay solution. This assay is able to detect ACP level as lower as 0.8 U/L, and covers a broad linear scope of 100.0 U/L. This work reports redox-responsive property of CuNCs and its underlying nature due to the oxidation of its interior copper atoms, and provides a sensitive assay method for ACP activity which is sufficiently sensitive for practical measurement in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs), consisting of a few to tens of copper atoms, have gained great attention in recent years because of its inherent merits including excellent luminescence, straightforward synthetic accessibility and significantly low cost in comparison to silver and gold nanoclusters [1,2]. A variety of biomacromolecules as the stabilizing ligands, such as DNA oligonucleotides, proteins and peptides, have been used to prepare CuNCs for monitoring biomolecules of interest [3–11]. Several polymers were also utilized to synthesize colorful CuNCs with the assistance of some specific reductants for sensing metal ions [12]. However, these CuNCs capped with macromolecules or polymers generally possess low emission efficiency, and are not readily accessible to external stimuli like pH and heat. Recent advances on thiol-containing small molecules stabilized CuNCs demonstrated that these CuNCs can be synthesized in a straightforward and facile fashion, and possess unique aggregation-induced emission (AIE) property and specific response to environmental changes [13–18]. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) is a new photophysical phenomenon first discovered by Tang's group in 2001, and possesses completely distinct emission behavior from traditional aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) process [19]. In AIE process, AIEgens emit very weak or no light in the dissolved or dispersed state, but become highly emissive when they are aggregated or in solid state. Recent advances indicate that restriction of intramolecular motions mainly contribute to the specific AIE behavior [20]. The similar AIE behaviors found for thiolated copper, silver and gold nanoclusters enable them to switch the luminescence in response to changes in pH value, solvent composition or external temperature [21]. However, redox characteristic, as one major distinction of CuNCs from the other metal nanoclusters, of CuNCs has been rarely examined and utilized for sensing applications.

Acid phosphatases (ACP, EC 3.1.3.2) are a family of enzymes that are widespread in nature and function to effectively remove a phosphatate group from its substrate under weakly acidic conditions [22]. Since an abnormal rise or decline in ACP level in human body is proved to be closely related to some diseases such as prostate cancer, Gaucher's disease, prostate malfunction and multiple myeloma, it has become a significant prognostic indicator and serum biomarker for medical diagnosis and drug screening [23–26]. As a result, it is of practical significance and in great demand to develop the more sensitive and convenient methods to precisely monitor ACP level. Up to date, most efforts have been devoted to optical detection of ACP level including traditional colorimetric assays based on quantifying absorbance of hydrolysis products [27–29]. However, recent attention has been focused on the development of fluorometric assays for ACP because of its higher sensitivity and wider choice of available molecular and nanoscale fluorophores. Except the early assays based on fluorogenic substrates [30–32], two general detection strategies are employed to quantify ACP level: (1) Electrostatic attraction-caused fluorescence quenching of different indicators like cationic molecular dyes [33,34], positively charge polymer [35] and CuInS2 quantum dots [36], is used to design the ACP assays; (2) Fluorescence switch induced by competitive coordination to metal cations is also utilized to determine ACP activity using a variety of fluorophores such as carbon quantum dots [37], gold nanoclusters [38], fluorescent polymers [39] and CuInS2 quantum dots [40]. Despite some of these preceding assays possess good analytical performance in quantification of ACP level, the former assays based on electrostatic force are readily impacted by ionic strengths of tested solutions while the latter assays mediated by metal cations are often subjected to binding stability between metal ions and the indicators and its complicate quenching mechanism. As a result, it is still a challenging task to develop reliable and sensitive ACP assays avoiding significant interference from complex matrices in terms of novel sensing strategies and luminescent materials.

In this study, multi-stimuli responsive copper nanoclusters with brightly red luminescence were synthesized via a facile and green approach, and its unique redox-responsive behavior in response to external redox stimuli was further designed to establish a novel ACP assay based on potential-controlled signal transduction. d-Penicillamine as the reductant and capping ligand was chosen to synthesize copper nanoclusters, and its synthesis conditions were optimized to prepare CuNCs with brightest luminescence. As-prepared CuNCs possess similar aggregation-induced emission property to the other thiolated CuNCs, and its sensitive responses to pH and environmental temperature were examined in detail and further related to its AIE property. It is interestingly found that as-prepared CuNC aggregates in acidic solution have unique redox-responsive behavior because the presence of some ions such as ferric and silver cations or the existence of external high potentials induced the entire luminescence quenching. This CuNCs-based luminescence switch controlled by external potentials was elucidated and further employed to establish a new ACP assay with resistance to environmental interference. The influence of substrate amount on the analytical performance of ACP assay was evaluated, and complex matrix effect was also assessed for practical measurement of ACP level in real samples.

Triple-distilled water was used throughout the experimental process. Copper sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O), acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), sodium pyrophosphate (Na4P2O7), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4·12H2O), iron(III) chloride (FeCl3·6H2O) and other inorganic salts were purchased from J&K Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). d-Penicillamine and acid phosphatase (ACP, EC 3.1.3.2, 2 kU) from potato were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Company (Shanghai, China). HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 5.0) was prepared by mixing stock solutions of NaAc (0.2 M) and HAc (0.2 M). Fe(PPi)2 – solution (5.0 mM) was prepared by mixing equivalent volume of Na4P2O7 (20.0 mM) and FeCl3 (10.0 mM) solution.

In a typical procedure, d-penicillamine (0.04 g) and CuSO4·5H2O (0.0125 g) were added into 10 mL of water in a 50-mL centrifuge tube. The solution was vigorously stirred at room temperature for 30 min, and then a milky and turbid solution was formed. The appearance of bright red luminescence under UV lamp indicated the completion of the synthesis. In order to remove extra reactants such as penicillamine and CuSO4, the resulting solution containing CuNCs was further centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the precipitate was washed with water three times. The resultant penicillamine-capped CuNCs were collected and then freeze-dried under vacuum, and as-prepared white powder was stored in the refrigerator for long-term preservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CuNCs-based luminescence switches controlled by pH and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The pH effect on the luminescence of CuNCs was performed using 0.4 mg/mL of CuNCs at variable pHs in the range of 4.0–8.0, where the pH of the solution was adjusted by NaOH or HCl solution (0.01 M). The reversibility of the luminescence switch was tested between pH 4.0 and pH 6.8, and the required pH was adjusted five times in a similar way. Temperature-dependent luminescence of CuNCs was recorded using 0.4 mg/mL of CuNCs in the range of 0.0–40.0 °C with the interval of 5 °C, and its reversibility was assessed between 0.0 °C and 40.0 °C five times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence quenching of CuNCs by ferric ions
ce:section.ce:para: For optimization of incubation time for luminescence quenching by Fe3+, 28 μL of Fe3+ solution (10.0 mM) was added into 3.0 mL of CuNCs (0.4 mg/mL) buffer solution (HAc-NaAc, pH 5.0). Then, the luminescence of mixed solution was recorded after different incubation times of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7 min, respectively. Under the optimum incubation time (4 min), the luminescence spectra of CuNC aggregates (0.4 mg/mL) in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0.0–93.3 μM) were recorded at the optimal excitation wavelength of 300 nm. All experiments were conducted at 37 °C.

For the selectivity assessment to Fe3+, seventeen metal ions including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ce2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Ag+ and Fe3+, were selected. A certain amount of each metal ion (93.3 μM) was added into 3.0 mL of CuNCs (0.4 mg/mL) solution, and then the luminescence spectra of CuNC aggregates solution was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 300 nm. HAc-NaAc solution (pH 5.0) was used as the buffer solution.

The solution containing CuNC aggregate (0.2 mg/mL) and NaCl (1.0 M) at pH 5.0 was first prepared in an electrolysis cell. The cyclic voltammetry curve of the resulting solution was recorded at applied potential range from −0.4 (vs SCE) to 0.6 V (vs SCE). To examine the influence of applied potentials on the luminescence of CuNC aggregate at pH 5.0, three applied potentials including 0.8 V (vs SCE), 0.4 V (vs SCE) and 0.0 V (vs SCE) were chosen. The luminescence spectra of CuNC aggregate at pH 5.0 were monitored after different periods of electrolysis (0 s, 300 s, 600 s, 900 s, 1200 s) for each applied potential.

The ACP activity was first assessed as extending incubation time with a certain amount of ACP level. The sensing system containing CuNC aggregate (0.4 mg/mL), Fe(PPi)2 – (93.3 μM), and ACP (380.0 U/L) in HAc-NaAc solution (pH 5.0) was monitored by luminescence every 2 min in an overall period of 20 min. Under the optimal incubation time (12 min), different levels of ACP ranging from 0.0 to 380.0 U/L was, respectively, added into 3.0 mL of assay solution (pH 5.0) containing CuNC aggregate (0.4 mg/mL) and Fe(PPi)2 – (93.3 μM). After an incubation time of 12 min at 37 °C, the luminescence spectra of the assay solution were recorded, respectively. Another two assays were performed using 45.0 and 153.0 μM of Fe(PPi)2 – in the same way, respectively. Matrix effect on the ACP assay was further assessed using 150-fold dilution of newborn calf serum as the complex matrix. The assay solution with complex matrix was first prepared by adding 20.0 μL of calf serum into the mixture containing CuNC aggregate (0.4 mg/mL) and Fe(PPi)2 – (93.3 μM). Different amounts of ACP in the range of 5.5–45.5 U/L were introduced into the preceding assay solution respectively, and then the resulting solution was monitored by fluorescence spectrophotometer after 12 min of incubation at the excitation wavelength of 300 nm.

The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. The luminescence spectra and time-resolved luminescence decay tests were performed using Edinburgh Instruments Model FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEOL-Model 2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. A Kratos Axis ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses. All electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI 660C electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co. Shanghai, China). A conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell was used with a glass carbon electrode (GCE, diameter 3.0 mm) as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as the counter electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of ACP level detection based on redox-responsive CuNC aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: d-Penicillamine was adopted to synthesize CuNCs as both the reductant and capping ligand, and as-prepared CuNCs possess unique aggregation-induced emission (AIE) property. CuNCs in its aggregated state show bright red luminescence upon the radiation of UV light; however, the luminescence of CuNCs dispersed in water almost completely disappears. As shown in Scheme 1 , change in physical state of CuNCs in water determines the switching of luminescence due to its specific AIE behavior, and the difference in pH value and temperature further controls the shifting between aggregated state and dispersed state. As a result, the luminescence of CuNCs is responsive to the change in pH or external temperature. More interestingly, brightly luminescent CuNC aggregates at low pHs sensitively respond to the presence of oxidative stimuli including high oxidizing potentials and commonly used oxidants such as Fe3+, Ag+ and H2O2. In terms of this specific redox-responsive nature of CuNC aggregates, a novel redox-mediated ACP assay was established. The complex Fe(PPi)2 – formed from Fe(III) and pyrophosphate ions, which exerts no impact on the luminescence of CuNC aggregates due to its low oxidizing potential, was used as the substrate of acid phosphatase. The presence of acid phosphatase enables pyrophosphate ions in Fe(PPi)2 – to rapidly hydrolyze into phosphate and free ferric ions, and the accumulation of free ferric ions prompts the oxidization of copper atoms in CuNC aggregates by ferric ions, where an electron transfer from CuNC aggregates to Fe(III) takes place as a redox reaction. The following luminescence quenching occurs because parts of copper atoms in the aggregate are oxidized to Cu(II) with higher valence. The ACP level is indirectly correlated to the luminescence quenching efficiency of CuNC aggregates, which is used to establish a quantitative detection assay for ACP activity.

Since it is reported that thiolate-protected copper nanoclusters have well-defined molecular structures, intriguing molecular-like properties and distinctive AIE behavior [21], d-penicillamine containing one thiol group was adopted to synthesize penicillamine-capped CuNCs where d-pencillamine serves as both the reductant and capping ligand. The synthesis proceeds fast and can be readily followed by naked eyes through observing the luminescence of the mixture upon radiation of UV light. To obtain the brightest luminescent CuNCs, the molar ratio of initial reactants including CuSO4 and penicillamine was first optimized in the range of 1:2 to 1:6. From Fig. S1, it is noted that as-obtained CuNCs in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 5.0) shows almost no luminescence when the molar ratio of CuSO4 to penicillamine is 1:2; intensely red-emitting CuNCs are generated as long as more than three equivalents of penicillamine to that of CuSO4 are used, and the brightest luminescence is recorded for CuNCs from initial ratio of 1:4. This great difference in luminescence intensity clearly suggests that the ratio of initial precursors could exert significant influence on the luminescence performance of CuNCs. As a result, CuNCs with brightest red luminescence from the optimal ratio of reactants were used for the following characterization and application. The formation of CuNCs is also readily verified from the comparison in UV–visible spectrum between CuNCs and penicillamine as shown in Fig. S2, where apparent absorption in the region of 250–700 nm is observed for CuNCs whereas only significant absorption at the wavelengths of lower than 250 nm occurs for penicillamine. The CuNCs dispersion in buffer solution exhibits bright red luminescence under a UV lamp, and more interestingly intense luminescence of CuNCs powder is also observed, clearly illustrating that as-prepared CuNCs possess AIE property. Their luminescence spectra in Fig. 1 A display that CuNCs dispersion in water and CuNCs powder have almost identical emission peak at around 690 nm but their optimum excitation wavelengths are different by nearly 100 nm. In terms of their time-resolved luminescence decay curves in Fig. 1B, their lifetimes are determined to be 25.64 and 20.88 μs respectively, and thus both of them probably belong to phosphorescence in terms of the time duration of the emission. The quantum yields of CuNCs with different concentrations in the range of 0.2–0.8 mg/mL at pH 5.0 varies from 8.2% to 8.9% using rhodamine 6G (QY 0.95 in ethanol) as standard reference. The morphology and size distribution of CuNCs were further characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), and its composition was also revealed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. TEM image of CuNCs with bright luminescence in Fig. 1C shows that the diameters of monodisperse CuNCs fall in the range of 3–6 nm, but most of them are larger than 10 nm in size, indicating that they are in the aggregated state. This aggregation state is responsible for strong luminescence of CuNCs due to its AIE characteristic. XPS analysis in Fig. 1D illustrates that as-prepared CuNCs are mainly composed of five elements including C, N, O, S and Cu; eight dominant peaks at 74.5, 121.6, 161.2, 225.9, 282.8, 401.9, 533.6 and 932.9 eV are assigned to binding energies of Cu 3p, Cu 3s, S 2p, S 2s, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Cu 2p in CuNCs, respectively. Semi-quantitative analysis in XPS indicates that CuNCs consist of 8.4% S, 61.6% C, 8.8% N, 16.8% O and 4.4% Cu, and molar ratio of Cu atoms to penicillamine in CuNCs is about 1:2, indicating that additional amount of penicillamine is required to reduce copper ions. High-resolution XPS in Fig. S3 shows two distinct peaks at 932.5 and 952.3 eV derived from Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 and no signal at around 942 eV from Cu(II), providing solid evidence for the fact that copper atoms in CuNCs exist in its lower valence state Cu0/Cu+ rather than Cu2+. The stability of CuNC aggregates dispersed in water in ambient environment was assessed because CuNCs with lower valence might be sensitive to oxygen. Fig. S4 shows that only very little change in luminescence can be observed after exposed to oxygen for 4 h, and more than half intensity is retained even after 27 h' exposure to air, suggesting that CuNC aggregates in water is stable within an appreciable time. The stability of CuNCs was also supported by the fact that the luminescence of CuNCs powder is remained even after several months' storage at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AIE-based luminescent nanoswitches of CuNCs controlled by pH and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: It has been found that the luminescence performance of CuNCs mainly depends on its aggregation state due to its inherent AIE property, and its physical state is further subjected to external pH values and environmental temperature [16,17]. The pH effect on the luminescence of CuNCs was examined as shown in Fig. 2 A, where the luminescence of CuNCs is gradually intensified as the decrease in pH value from 8.0 to 4.0. This appreciable luminescence increase is attributed to the enhancement of aggregation degree of CuNCs at low pHs, and significant dispersion of CuNCs in alkaline solution due to deprotonation of carboxyl group in penicillamine is responsible for the weakening of the luminescence. The luminescence of CuNCs is readily controlled by pH switching between pH 4.0 and pH 6.8, and its reversibility of this nanoswitch is displayed in Fig. 2B. After five cycles, the difference in luminescence between the two pH values is not apparently reduced, indicating that this nanoswitch has excellent reversibility. Although thermo-responsive behavior of glutathione-protected CuNCs has been reported [17], the variation of the capping ligand could exert a significant impact on its responsive capability to external temperature. Fig. 2C shows that CuNCs in buffer solution at 0 °C exhibit the brightest luminescence, and its luminescence is progressively quenched as the rise of the temperature from 0 °C to 40 °C. The sensitive response in luminescence intensity to environmental temperature is originated from its AIE behavior and temperature-dependent solubility of CuNCs in water. Major difference in thermo-responsibility between penicillamine-capped CuNCs and glutathione-protected CuNCs is that the response sensitivity of the former is much higher than the latter, and the former is more suited for sensing temperature in living cells because the luminescence of CuNCs in this work almost entirely quenched when the temperature is at around the optimal temperature for cell growth. The reversibility of the nanoswitch in response to environmental temperature was also assessed in Fig. 2D, which shows that the luminescence can be shifted between on and off in response to 0 °C and 40 °C for five cycles in a reversible manner. As a result, CuNCs in this work are capable to serve as the pH indicator and the nanothermometer in vitro and in vivo.

Since it has been reported that several cations such as Fe3+ and Cu2+ are capable to effectively quench the luminescence of certain nanomaterials including gold nanoclusters [38], CuInS2 quantum dots [40] and carbon quantum dots [41] via metal ion-induced aggregation or the formation of nonfluorescent metal complexes, the impact of commonly used metal cations on the luminescence of CuNCs aggregate was assessed. Fig. S5 displays the comparison in luminescence quenching efficiency [(I0-I)/I0] induced by different metal ions including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Ag+ and Fe3+. Among these seventeen metal cations, Fe3+, Ag+ and Cu2+ induce significant luminescence quenching to CuNC aggregate and the highest quenching efficiency by Fe3+ is up to 80% while the others only exert very small impact on the luminescence. The underlying nature of luminescence quenching by these three metal ions is further explored taking Fe3+ as an example. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the luminescence of CuNC aggregate is gradually quenched as the increase of Fe3+ in amount, and most luminescence is quenched when the concentration of Fe3+ is 93.3 μM. The values I0/I and τ0/τ versus the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0.0–25.0 μM were also plotted in Fig. 3B. The relationship between value I0/I and τ0/τ versus the concentration of Fe3+ is linear, and can be expressed as I0/I = 0.984 + 0.0124[Fe3+] with R2 = 0.995, while the values for τ0/τ from time-resolved decay curves in Fig. S6 are nearly constant as the change in concentration of ferric ions. This result clearly indicates that this quenching induced by Fe3+ belongs to static quenching. Two possibilities are responsible to this static quenching: (1) ferric cations could bind with capping ligand of CuNC aggregate to form non-luminescent complexes, and this phenomenon has been used to design chemo/biosensors [38,40]; (2) copper atoms inside CuNCs could be oxidized by ferric ions to create non-luminescent CuNCs containing Cu(II), and the presence of Cu(II) inside CuNCs changes its luminescent nature. The first hypothesis for the formation of non-luminescent complexes was tested in Fig. 3C as follows. After the addition of Fe3+ into CuNC aggregate solution, the luminescence is almost completely quenched. If the first hypothesis worked, the introduction of two equivalents of pyrophosphate ions should cause the recovery of the luminescence because pyrophosphate ions have stronger affinity to ferric ions to produce Fe(PPi)2 – complex. However, the fact that the luminescence remains quenched after adding 186.6 μM PPi ions clearly indicates that the first hypothesis can be ruled out. The small influence on the luminescence of CuNCs by Fe(PPi)2 – complex implies effective oxidation of copper atoms inside CuNCs by strong oxidant like free ferric ions probably contributes this severe quenching. The cyclic voltammetry test on CuNC aggregate in buffer solution was performed to determine its oxidation potential. One can note from CV graph in Fig. 3D that the oxidation potential of CuNCs versus the potential of calomel electrode is 0.124 V, i.e., its standard oxidation potential vs SHE is about 0.37 V, which is close to the standard potential (0.34 V) for the conversion of Cu2+ to Cu(s). It is much lower than reduction potentials of Fe3+ (0.77 V), Ag+ (0.80 V) and H2O2 (1.78 V), and thereby copper atoms in CuNCs can be readily oxidized by the preceding oxidants. To further verify the oxidation-cased luminescence quenching, the electrolysis of CuNC aggregates in HAc-NaAc buffer solution at different potentials were performed. Fig. 4 displays that most luminescence of CuNC aggregates is quenched after 1200 s’ electrolysis when the potentials of 0.4 (vs. SCE) and 0.8 V (vs. SCE) were applied, and the electrolysis at the higher applied potential causes a faster quenching of the luminescence; however, its luminescence almost remains unchanged after the same duration of electrolysis at 0.0 V (vs. SCE). These observations strongly support that luminescence quenching is originated from the oxidation of copper atoms inside CuNCs when an external potential larger than 0.124 V (vs. SCE) is applied or a strong oxidant is present. As a result, the formation of Cu(II) from the oxidation of copper atoms with lower valence in CuNCs is responsible for redox-responsive behavior of CuNC aggregates, which enables CuNC aggregates to be sensitive response to external potentials and specific oxidants such as ferric ions. This redox-mediated luminescence property of CuNC aggregates can be used to design novel chemo/biosensors by controlling the external potentials. Luminescence quenching trends of CuNC aggregates by Ag+ and H2O2 were displayed in Fig. S7, and the detection limits for Ag+, Fe3+ and H2O2 were determined to be 1.1 μM, 0.75 μM and 5.3 μM respectively, according to their respective calibration curve in Fig. S8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time luminescent assay for acid phosphatase based on redox-responsive CuNC aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: In terms of the luminescent response of CuNC aggregates to specific oxidation potentials, a novel detection strategy for ACP activity was designed as shown in Scheme 1. This detection strategy depends on the following facts: (1) the standard potential of Fe(PPi)2 – is much lower than the potential for the conversion of Cu(II) from Cu atoms in CuNCs so that the luminescence of CuNC aggregates could not be substantially influenced by the presence of Fe(PPi)2 –; (2) ACP is capable to catalyze the hydrolysis of PPi ions into phosphate ions, which causes the rapid breakdown of Fe(PPi)2 – and the creation of a large number of free ferric ions; (3) the potential of free ferric ions produced from the preceding hydrolysis is sufficiently high to effectively oxidize the Cu atoms in CuNCs, which finally induces the luminescence quenching. The feasibility to measure ACP activity based on this proposed detection route was first assessed as shown in Fig. 5 A. Only about 12% of luminescence quenching of CuNC aggregates is attained in the presence of 93.3 μM of Fe(PPi)2 –; however, a quenching efficiency of more than 72% is observed after the introduction of the same amount of Fe3+ to Fe(PPi)2 –. Moreover, the coexistence of four equivalents of phosphate ions with Fe3+ still leads to the almost same degree of luminescence quenching, indicating the presence of phosphate ions in such a large amount does not significantly lower the potential of Fe3+. This large difference in quenching efficiency between Fe(PPi)2 – and the mixture containing Fe3+ and phosphate ions provides the possibility to measure ACP level, which is confirmed by the sharp decline in luminescence after the addition of 380.0 U/L of ACP into the mixture containing CuNC aggregates and Fe(PPi)2 –. As a result, it is feasible to measure ACP level based on this detection strategy in a real-time way.

Since the optimum pH for ACP is about 5.0 [22], the stability test on the luminescence of CuNC aggregates at pH 5.0 was first performed. Results in Fig. S9 show that the luminescence of CuNC aggregates in pH 5.0 buffer solution reach stable after a very short time of incubation (3 min), indicating this equilibrium controlled by pH can be attained rapidly and well maintained. Fig. 5B shows the optimization of incubation time for luminescence quenching in the presence of a fixed amount of Fe(PPi)2 – and different ACP levels. It is found that the luminescence using two different ACP levels including 180.0 and 380.0 U/L is rapidly quenched within 10 min of incubation time and remains almost unchanged after 12 min of incubation, suggesting 12 min is sufficient to attain the equilibrium. The sensing performance of the system containing CuNCs and Fe(PPi)2 – was conducted with the introduction of different levels of ACP under optimum conditions, and the luminescence intensity is gradually decreased as ACP level is increased from 0.0 to 380.0 U/L. Fig. 5C clearly demonstrates the growth trend of the quenching efficiency as elevating the ACP level where a sharp rise at the first stage from 0.0 to 100.0 U/L is followed by a smooth decline for the range over 100.0 U/L. Fig. 5D shows luminescence quenching efficiency as a function of ACP level and a good linear relationship between the luminescence quenching efficiency and ACP level in the range from 2.2 to 45.5 U/L. The regression equation can be expressed as y = 0.0049x–0.0041, where R2 is 0.995. The detection limit using three times standard deviations is determined to be 0.8 U/L. The influence of the amount of Fe(PPi)2 – on the analytical performance of the ACP assay was further examined by using another two concentrations for Fe(PPi)2 – instead of the original one. Similar decline trends in luminescence as the rise of ACP level were observed for both of the assays using 45.0 and 153.0 μM of Fe(PPi)2 – as shown in Figs. S10 and S11. However, appreciable differences in linear scope are readily noted from their respective fitting curve although there are only small variations in detection limit varying from 0.8 to 1.7 U/L. The narrowest linear scope ranging from 3.1 to 16.0 U/L was found for the assay using 45.0 μM of Fe(PPi)2 –, while the assay using 153.0 μM of Fe(PPi)2 – possesses the broadest linear range of up to 100.0 U/L. These data suggest that the choice of the amount of Fe(PPi)2 – in the assay is an important factor for its analytical performance, and the use of larger amount of Fe(PPi)2 – is desirable for the samples containing high levels of ACP. Table S1 summaries luminescent assays for ACP activity in terms of luminogen, substrate and detection limit. One can note that the detection limit of ACP assay in this work is much better than those of most reported methods such as carbon quantum dots-based assay (5.5 U/L) [37], AuNCs-based method (1.0 U/L) [38], and molecular dye-based probe (4.9 U/L) [33,34]. Our assay is sufficiently sensitive for practical measurement of ACP level in human serum and seminal plasma in comparison to its normal level in blood (35–123 U/L) [42] and seminal plasma (30–36 U/mL) [23]. In addition, 150-fold dilution of newborn calf serum was used to quantitatively assess the interference from complex matrix by standard addition method. Six standard added values in the range of 0.0–40.5 U/L were selected according to the ACP assay using 93.3 μM of Fe(PPi)2 –. Table S2 summaries the recovery ratios of measured value to added value for six samples, and the recovery ratio varies from 94.8% to 98.2%, indicating that the complex matrix such as 150-fold dilution of calf serum exerts an insignificant interference on this assay, and this assay is capable to accurately measure ACP level in such a complex matrix as real samples. This ACP assay is established based on redox-responsive behavior of CuNC aggregates, and a novel signal transduction mechanism induced by the oxidation of CuNC aggregates was proposed. Despite a small interference from complex matrix is still observed, this ACP assay performs better in resistance to complex matrix as real serum than the previous assays which suffer from significant interference from complex matrix such as real serum samples [33–36,38–41].

In summary, we explore multi-stimuli responsive behaviors of copper nanoclusters, design three distinct luminescence nanoswitches in respective response to pH, temperature and specific redox species, and further establish a reliable ACP assay based on luminescence switch controlled by external potentials. It is found that the change in pH or temperature induces shift in its physical state between aggregate state and dispersion state, and further causes the switch of the luminescence of CuNCs. The pH/thermo-responsive behaviors are originated from the aggregation-induced emission property of CuNCs, and can be used as the pH indicator and the nanothermometer. The redox-responsive behavior of CuNC aggregates in sensitive response to specific redox species was revealed and attributed to the conversion of Cu(II) from copper atoms with lower valence inside CuNCs. This property allows the luminescence of CuNCs to be controlled by specific external potentials or redox species, and the distinctive responses to different redox species are used to design a novel detection strategy for ACP activity. The presence of Fe(PPi)2 complex exerts no impact on the luminescence of CuNC aggregates; however, the coexistence of Fe(PPi)2 complex and ACP induces the severe luminescence quenching because of the creation of Cu(II) inside CuNCs followed by the hydrolysis of pyrophosphate ions by ACP. The correlation between quenched luminescence and ACP level is used to establish the ACP assay in a real-time way. The detection limit can be lowered to 0.8 U/L depending on the amount of the substrate, and the sensitivity is sufficient to detect ACP level in human serum and seminal plasma. Our method employs a novel detection strategy mediated by redox reaction to avoid the significant interference from complex matrix. This work reveals multi-stimuli responsive behaviors of CuNCs for construction of multiple luminescence switches, demonstrates an example of redox-mediated detection strategy for ACP assay, and broadens the application of copper nanoclusters in bioanalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (Grant No. LY17B050003) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21675143 and 21405142).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online data Online data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.06.046.
